food_subgroup: Stir-fried diced chicken with roasted peanuts, dried red chillies, and Sichuan peppercorn in soy-rice wine-chilli sauce; restaurant serving ~250g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Kung Pao Chicken as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be a stir-fried poultry dish category; no exact URI available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried chicken with nuts and chilli sauce (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 entry for Kung Pao Chicken exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Kung Pao Chicken as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Kung Pao Chicken is approximately 250g, providing roughly 380 kcal, 28g protein, 22g fat, and 18g carbohydrate, with approximately 900mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Kung Pao Chicken — Component Calculation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient entries (chicken thigh, peanuts, soy sauce, vegetable oil, chilli, cornstarch)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (chicken thigh meat, roasted peanuts, soy sauce, vegetable oil, dried red chilli, cornstarch, garlic, ginger). No single USDA entry for Kung Pao Chicken as a composite dish exists; values represent a weighted blend consistent with a standard Sichuan restaurant recipe at ~250g serving. Per-100g values scaled accordingly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes — Analogous Estimate: protein- and fat-dominant stir-fried chicken dish with peanuts; closest analogous GI reference from University of Sydney GI Database for high-protein, high-fat Chinese mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kung Pao Chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~35 (low) based on analogous high-protein, high-fat Chinese stir-fry dishes. The peanut MUFA content significantly attenuates glycaemic response; the sauce sugar (~12g per serving) is moderated by co-ingested fat and protein. GL per 250g serving = (35 × 18g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.3 (low). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~900mg per 250g serving; ~360mg per 100g) primarily from soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit or avoid Kung Pao Chicken in its standard restaurant preparation due to ~900mg sodium per serving (38–45% of the 2000mg daily limit); requesting reduced soy sauce can lower sodium to approximately 600mg per serving, which is a meaningfully safer hypertensive profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load (~6.3 per serving) driven by high protein from chicken and MUFA from peanuts
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kung Pao Chicken is among the most Type 2 Diabetes-compatible Sichuan restaurant dishes, with GI ~35 and GL ~6 per serving; the peanut MUFA and chicken protein substantially blunt postprandial glucose response, and the dish can be included in a T2DM meal plan with attention to the ~12g sauce sugar per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable MUFA fat profile from peanuts offset by high sodium and moderate cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The peanut-derived MUFA (~10g per serving) supports LDL-C reduction and is cardiovascularly beneficial, but the high sodium content (~900mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol (~105mg per serving) require caution for patients with established cardiovascular disease; choosing reduced-sodium preparation and limiting portion size to 200g is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA from peanuts and bioactive compounds from garlic and ginger
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kung Pao Chicken has a nutritionally favourable anti-inflammatory profile from peanut MUFA (oleic acid), garlic quercetin and allicin (mild COX-2 inhibition), and ginger; the primary RA concern is the high sodium (~900mg per serving) which may exacerbate fluid retention in patients on corticosteroids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kung Pao Chicken (宫保鸡丁, Gōngbǎo Jīdīng) is a world-renowned stir-fry dish originating from Sichuan Province, China, named after Qing dynasty official Ding Baozhen (丁宝桢), Governor of Sichuan, whose honorary title was 宫保 (Gōngbǎo). The dish features tender diced chicken thigh stir-fried at high heat in a wok with roasted peanuts, dried red chillies, and Sichuan peppercorns, finished in a savory-sweet-sour glaze of soy sauce, rice wine, black vinegar, sugar, and cornstarch. The flavour profile is a complex 'mala' (numbing-spicy) and 'lychee-flavour' (sweet-sour) balance unique to Sichuan cuisine. Texturally, the dish contrasts tender chicken with crunchy peanuts and aromatic dried chillies. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 250g, providing ~380 kcal, 28g protein, 22g fat (peanut MUFA-dominant, anti-inflammatory), 18g carbohydrate, and ~900mg sodium. GI is estimated at ~35 (low) with GL ~6.3 per serving, making it one of the most Type 2 Diabetes-compatible Chinese restaurant main dishes. The high sodium content from soy sauce is the primary concern for hypertensive patients. Peanut MUFA (~10g per serving) supports cardiovascular health by reducing LDL-C. Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient entries; no single approved-source entry for Kung Pao Chicken as a composite dish exists. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to a high-protein, high-fat Chinese stir-fry. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are derived by component calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (chicken thigh, roasted peanuts, soy sauce, vegetable oil, dried red chilli, cornstarch, garlic, ginger) scaled to a standard Sichuan restaurant recipe at ~250g serving. No single USDA or HPB entry for Kung Pao Chicken as a composite dish exists. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite prepared dish is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation; detailed moisture data not reported in the component calculation source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.4g/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for chicken thigh and roasted peanuts in proportion to recipe composition; confidence 0.85. 4. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data — no hydrogenated fats used in standard Sichuan Kung Pao Chicken preparation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for chicken thigh and peanut components; this is a naturally near-zero value for this dish type. 6. GI ~35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Kung Pao Chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Sodium per serving (~900mg) is calculated as 360mg/100g × 2.5 (250g serving). 8. Duplicate portion entries in input (two identical '1 serving 250g' entries) consolidated to one default serving of 250g. 9. FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 entry for Kung Pao Chicken exists. 10. This dish is not inherently halal-certified as rice wine (Shaoxing wine) is a standard ingredient; halal versions substitute with chicken broth.
